chunk_summary: Post-discharge nutrition for preterm/LBW/ELBW infants with cholestasis requires tailored fat-soluble vitamin supplementation, monitoring for deficiencies/toxicities, and specialist oversight to address fat malabsorption and growth concerns.
learning_objectives: Understand the impact of cholestasis on fat-soluble vitamin absorption in preterm/LBW/ELBW infants post-discharge.
Identify appropriate dosing ranges and formulations for vitamins A, D, E, and K in cholestasis management.
Recognize the importance of monitoring clinical and biochemical markers to prevent vitamin deficiencies or toxicities.
Learn strategies to optimize growth and nutrition, including the use of human milk and specialized formulas.
key_facts: Cholestasis causes fat malabsorption, leading to early deficiencies in vitamins A, D, E, and K.
Recommended dosing ranges: vitamin A (3,000–5,000 IU/day), vitamin D (1,000–2,000 IU/day), vitamin E (adjusted to weight/levels), and vitamin K1 (twice weekly to daily based on INR/PT).
Water-miscible formulations of fat-soluble vitamins are preferred for better absorption.
Monitoring includes INR/PT, serum 25-hydroxyvitamin D, and vitamins A and E levels where available.
Persistent poor growth, fractures, or abnormal phosphorus/ALP levels may indicate metabolic bone disease requiring further evaluation.
Specialized formulas with medium-chain triglycerides may benefit some infants under specialist guidance.
reference_quote: Prefer water-miscible (aqueous) formulations of fat-soluble vitamins when available. Practical dosing ranges used in pediatric cholestasis guidance include: vitamin A about 3,000–5,000 IU/day, vitamin D about 1,000–2,000 IU/day (higher only with monitoring), vitamin E dosing in the tens of IU/day adjusted to levels and weight, and vitamin K1 dosing from twice weekly to daily depending on INR/PT and bleeding risk.
document_key_topics: Monitoring and management of vitamin deficiencies and toxicities
Specialized nutrition strategies for growth and absorption
